title: An age of reform
description: Test your skills on why several reform movements started in the United States from 1800 to 1848

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In a time of rapidly changing means of communication and systems of production, when everything from race relations to banking practices came under challenge, there was no sharp distinction between the mainstream and the marginal. The utopians simply carried even further the perfectionism that mainstream evangelists like Charles Finney reached. Typically, they did not so much reject American society as wish to elaborate upon it, to carry its innovative qualities to extremes. Their communities attracted attention out of all proportion to their size.” 

> -Source: Daniel Walker Howe, *What Hath God Wrought: The Transformation of America, 1815-1848,* 2007

**Which of the following had the most significant impact on the creation of utopian societies as described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the technological and production changes following the Market Revolution
the expansion of suffrage to all white people during the Jacksonian Era
the influence of the War of 1812 during the Era of Good Feelings
the creation of new reform movements after the Second Great Awakening

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In a time of rapidly changing means of communication and systems of production, when everything from race relations to banking practices came under challenge, there was no sharp distinction between the mainstream and the marginal. The utopians simply carried even further the perfectionism that mainstream evangelists like Charles Finney reached. Typically, they did not so much reject American society as wish to elaborate upon it, to carry its innovative qualities to extremes. Their communities attracted attention out of all proportion to their size.” 

> -Source: Daniel Walker Howe, *What Hath God Wrought: The Transformation of America, 1815-1848,* 2007

**Which of the following contributed most directly to the trend described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the spread of religious ideals following the Second Great Awakening
the development of political parties after the ratification of the Constitution
the rise in national debt following involvement in the War of 1812
the change in gender roles after the extension of suffrage to women

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In a time of rapidly changing means of communication and systems of production, when everything from race relations to banking practices came under challenge, there was no sharp distinction between the mainstream and the marginal. The utopians simply carried even further the perfectionism that mainstream evangelists like Charles Finney reached. Typically, they did not so much reject American society as wish to elaborate upon it, to carry its innovative qualities to extremes. Their communities attracted attention out of all proportion to their size.” 

> -Source: Daniel Walker Howe, *What Hath God Wrought: The Transformation of America, 1815-1848,* 2007

**According to the excerpt, which of the following statements describes why people sought to build utopian communities in the 1800s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to set up a self-sufficient society based on perfectionism
to create a trail for enslaved people to escape their captors
to treat people diagnosed with a mental illness
to give non-Christians a safe place to worship

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3958a741243f240a576ca3b1b0f10641.jpg)

>-Source: [“The Drunkards Progress. From the First Glass to the Grave.”](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/de/The_Drunkard%27s_Progress_1846.jpg), 1846

**The situation depicted in the image best serves as evidence of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise of a temperance movement
the introduction of legislation to limit immigration
the end of the influence of the Second Great Awakening
the start of a period of prohibition

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3958a741243f240a576ca3b1b0f10641.jpg)

>-Source: [“The Drunkards Progress. From the First Glass to the Grave.”](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/de/The_Drunkard%27s_Progress_1846.jpg), 1846

**Which of the following sentences best describes the purpose of this etching?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to depict drunkenness as a sin, encouraging people to abstain from drinking alcohol
to portray immigrants as immoral, encouraging Congress to limit immigration
to advertise the positive effects of drinking alcohol, encouraging healthy living
to promote the purchase of domestic alcohols, encouraging Americans to stop purchasing French wine

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3958a741243f240a576ca3b1b0f10641.jpg)

>-Source: [“The Drunkards Progress. From the First Glass to the Grave.”](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/de/The_Drunkard%27s_Progress_1846.jpg), 1846

**Which of the following groups would have been most likely to support the message suggested in the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: white women
enslaved men
white men
immigrant women

assessment_items.question_content:
> “[W]e believe and affirm:
That every American citizen who retains a human being in involuntary bondage as his property is (according to Scripture) a MAN STEALER. 
That the slaves ought instantly to be set free. . . .
That all those laws which are now in force admitting the right of slavery, are . . ., before God, utterly null and void, being an audacious usurpation of the Divine prerogative. . . .
[T]hat no compensation should be given to the outraged and guiltless slaves and not to those who have plundered and abused them. 
[That] we concede the Congress under the resent national compact, has no right to interfere with any of the slave states, in relation to this momentous subject [slavery]. But we maintain that Congress has a right. . . to suppress the domestic slave trade between the slave states, and to abolish slavery in those portions of our territory which the Constitution has placed under its exclusive jurisdiction.”

> -Source: William Lloyd Garrison,  “Declaration of the National Anti-Slavery Convention,” 1834

**Which of the following groups of people would have been most likely to support Garrison’s views in the excerpt?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Northern white women
Southern white women
Plantation owners
Yeoman farmers

That the slaves ought instantly to be set free. . . .

[T]hat no compensation should be given to the outraged and guiltless slaves and not to those who have plundered and abused them. 
[That] we concede the Congress under the resent national compact, has no right to interfere with any of the slave states, in relation to this momentous subject [slavery]. But we maintain that Congress has a right. . . to suppress the domestic slave trade between the slave states, and to abolish slavery in those portions of our territory which the Constitution has placed under its exclusive jurisdiction.”

> -Source: William Lloyd Garrison,  “Declaration of the National Anti-Slavery Convention,” 1834

**Which of the following best describes the author’s strategy to get public support for their message?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: appeal to people’s religious values and morality
use Enlightenment ideals and rationalism
draw on scientific claims that argued some races were inferior
propose legislation at the state level rather than the federal level

assessment_items.question_content:
> “[W]e believe and affirm:
That every American citizen who retains a human being in involuntary bondage as his property is (according to Scripture) a MAN STEALER. 
That the slaves ought instantly to be set free. . . .
That all those laws which are now in force admitting the right of slavery, are . . ., before God, utterly null and void, being an audacious usurpation of the Divine prerogative. . . .
[T]hat no compensation should be given to the outraged and guiltless slaves and not to those who have plundered and abused them. 
[That] we concede the Congress under the resent national compact, has no right to interfere with any of the slave states, in relation to this momentous subject [slavery]. But we maintain that Congress has a right. . . to suppress the domestic slave trade between the slave states, and to abolish slavery in those portions of our territory which the Constitution has placed under its exclusive jurisdiction.”

> -Source: William Lloyd Garrison,  “Declaration of the National Anti-Slavery Convention,” 1834

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the influence of the Second Great Awakening on reform movements
the growing support of the abolition movement in the West and South
the success of the abolition movement in the 1800s to impact any change
the ways in which free black people organized to end slavery in the United States

assessment_items.question_content:
> “. . . But we are assembled to protest against a form of government, existing without the consent of the governed— to declare our right to be free as man is free, to be represented in the government which we are taxed to support, to have such disgraceful laws as give man the power to chastise and imprison his wife. . . . And, strange as it may seem to many, we now demand our right to vote according to the declaration of the government under which we live.”

>-Elizabeth Cady Stanton at the Seneca Falls Convention, “Declaration of Sentiments,” 1848

**The language and themes of the excerpt were most directly inspired by which of the following foundational documents?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Declaration of Independence
The Articles of Confederation
*The Federalist Papers,* No. 51
*The Federalist Papers,* No. 10

assessment_items.question_content:
> “. . . But we are assembled to protest against a form of government, existing without the consent of the governed— to declare our right to be free as man is free, to be represented in the government which we are taxed to support, to have such disgraceful laws as give man the power to chastise and imprison his wife. . . . And, strange as it may seem to many, we now demand our right to vote according to the declaration of the government under which we live.”

>-Elizabeth Cady Stanton at the Seneca Falls Convention, “Declaration of Sentiments,” 1848

**Arguments similar to those expressed in the excerpt were later employed to justify which of the following?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the creation of methods for birth control
the ratification of the Thirteenth Amendment
the passage of the Defense of Marriage Act
the election of Abraham Lincoln to the presidency

assessment_items.question_content:
> “. . . But we are assembled to protest against a form of government, existing without the consent of the governed— to declare our right to be free as man is free, to be represented in the government which we are taxed to support, to have such disgraceful laws as give man the power to chastise and imprison his wife. . . . And, strange as it may seem to many, we now demand our right to vote according to the declaration of the government under which we live.”

>-Elizabeth Cady Stanton at the Seneca Falls Convention, “Declaration of Sentiments,” 1848

**The author’s remarks in the excerpt most directly reflected which of the following developments during the early nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the start of a Women’s Rights Movement
the end of the influence of the Second Great Awakening
the start of a public school movement
the end of the “separate spheres” ideology
